walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Game Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Le Havre set up. First, place the large game board in the center of the table. This board has the harbor area with supply spaces along the top, and a space for buildings along the bottom. Make sure everyone can reach the board comfortably.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Supply Spaces
walkthrough.text: Look at the top of the game board. You will see seven supply spaces arranged in a row. Each space corresponds to a type of good that will arrive at the harbor. There is also a round arrow marker that tracks which supply space gets filled next. Place the arrow marker on its starting position.

walkthrough.title: Sort the Standard Buildings
walkthrough.text: Find the thirty-three standard building cards. These have a number printed on them and are the same in every game. Sort them by number and lay them face up in a row near the board. Everyone should be able to see what buildings are available to construct.

walkthrough.title: Deal the Special Buildings
walkthrough.text: Now find the thirty-six special building cards. These are the buildings that change every game and make each session feel different. Shuffle them thoroughly face down. Deal out a number of special buildings based on your player count and place them face up in a separate display near the standard buildings. Return the rest to the box without looking at them.

walkthrough.title: Place the Round Overview Cards
walkthrough.text: Find the seven round overview cards. These cards show which goods appear at the harbor each round and when ships become available. Arrange them in order from round one through round seven. Place them near the board where everyone can reference them throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Starting Buildings
walkthrough.text: Two buildings start the game already constructed and available to everyone. Find the Construction Firm and the Building Firm cards. Place these in the town area as neutral buildings that any player can use for free. These are the backbone of the early game.

walkthrough.title: Organize the Goods Supply
walkthrough.text: Sort all the goods tokens by type and place them near the board as a general supply. You will need piles for Francs, fish, wood, clay, iron, grain, and cattle. Also set out piles for the processed goods: smoked fish, charcoal, bricks, steel, bread, meat, leather, and coke. Keep them organized so players can easily find what they need.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Ships
walkthrough.text: Find all the ship cards or tokens. Sort them by type: wooden ships and iron ships. Place them near the board in their own area. Ships will become available for construction as the game progresses, according to the round overview cards.

walkthrough.title: Set Out the Loan Cards
walkthrough.text: Place the stack of loan cards near the board where everyone can reach them. Nobody wants to take loans, but they are there as a safety net. If you cannot feed your workers during a feeding round, you will be forced to take loans from this pile.

walkthrough.title: Determine Player Count Adjustments
walkthrough.text: Le Havre plays differently depending on player count. Check the rulebook or round cards for your specific player count to determine the number of rounds, turns per round, and how many special buildings to use. With fewer players, each player gets more turns but there are fewer total rounds.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Player Markers
walkthrough.text: Each player takes one player marker in their chosen color. This marker will be placed on buildings when you use them. You only have one marker, which means you can only occupy one building at a time. When you move to a new building, your marker comes with you.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Starting Resources
walkthrough.text: Each player receives their starting goods. For three players, everyone gets five Francs and one charcoal. For four or five players, everyone gets four Francs and one charcoal. Keep your goods visible in front of you. Your supply is always public information in Le Havre.

walkthrough.title: Determine First Player
walkthrough.text: Choose a starting player randomly. Play will proceed clockwise from the first player. In Le Havre, turn order matters quite a bit because goods accumulate on the supply spaces and the first player to grab a large pile gets a significant advantage.

walkthrough.title: Fill the Initial Supply
walkthrough.text: Before the first turn, add goods to the supply spaces according to the round one card. This gives players something to collect right from the start. The goods that appear depend on the round overview card for round one. Place the indicated goods tokens on their corresponding supply spaces on the board. You are now ready to play.

bullets: Place game board centrally with harbor supply spaces visible
Ensure all players can reach the board

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Le Havre is an economic engine-building game set in the French harbor city of Le Havre. Over the course of the game, you will collect goods that arrive at the harbor, use buildings to process raw materials into more valuable goods, construct buildings, and buy ships. The player with the most total wealth at the end of the game wins.

walkthrough.title: How Rounds Work
walkthrough.text: The game is played over a fixed number of rounds determined by player count. Each round has a set number of turns. On each turn, exactly one player acts. The number of total turns you get across the entire game is roughly twenty, regardless of player count. As the game progresses, more powerful buildings and ships become available.

walkthrough.title: Your Turn: Step One - Supply
walkthrough.text: Every turn starts the same way. Before you take your main action, you must advance the supply arrow one space forward. Then add the goods shown on that supply space to the corresponding offer space on the board. Goods accumulate on offer spaces if nobody takes them, so piles can grow quite large over several turns.

walkthrough.title: Your Turn: Step Two - Main Action
walkthrough.text: After adding supply, you must choose exactly one main action. You have two choices. You can take all the goods from one offer space, grabbing everything that has piled up there. Or you can use a building by placing your worker marker on it and using its ability. You must do one or the other. You cannot skip your action.

walkthrough.title: Taking Goods from an Offer Space
walkthrough.text: When you choose to take goods, you collect every single token from one offer space. You cannot take partial piles or split between spaces. This is all or nothing. The offer space is then empty and will start accumulating goods again on future turns. Timing is everything here. Taking a space with six wood is much better than taking one with two.

walkthrough.title: Using a Building
walkthrough.text: When you choose to use a building, place your worker marker on it and carry out its ability. You can use any building in the game: your own buildings, another player's buildings, or neutral town buildings. Buildings let you process goods, sell goods for Francs, construct new buildings, build ships, and more.

walkthrough.title: Entry Fees
walkthrough.text: Using your own buildings is always free. Using neutral town buildings that nobody owns is also free. But if you want to use a building owned by another player, you must pay them an entry fee. The entry fee is printed on each building card, usually one food or one Franc. This is paid directly to the building's owner.

walkthrough.title: Building Occupation
walkthrough.text: Only one worker marker can be on a building at a time. However, there is no permanent blocking in Le Havre. When a new player uses an occupied building, the previous player's marker is simply returned to them. So you can always use any building you want as long as you can pay any entry fee.

walkthrough.title: Processing Raw Goods
walkthrough.text: A major part of Le Havre is converting raw goods into processed goods worth more. Wood becomes charcoal at the Charburner. Clay becomes bricks at the Brickworks. Iron becomes steel at the Steel Mill, which also requires energy. Fish become smoked fish at the Smokehouse, also requiring energy. Grain becomes bread at the Bakehouse with energy. Cattle become meat and hides at the Abattoir.

walkthrough.title: Energy for Processing
walkthrough.text: Several processing buildings require energy to operate. Energy can come from charcoal, coke, or wood. When a building says it needs energy, you must pay the required energy tokens along with the raw goods you are converting. Charcoal and coke are the most efficient energy sources, while wood works in a pinch.

walkthrough.title: Constructing Buildings
walkthrough.text: To build a new building, you use the Construction Firm or Building Firm action. Pay the resource cost printed on the building card, which is usually a combination of wood, clay, iron, bricks, steel, or Francs. The building then goes into your personal area. You own it and score its value at game end.

walkthrough.title: Building Ownership Benefits
walkthrough.text: Owning a building gives you three advantages. First, its printed Franc value counts toward your final score. Second, you can use it for free whenever you want without paying an entry fee. Third, other players must pay you the entry fee whenever they use it. Buildings are investments that pay off in multiple ways.

walkthrough.title: Building Ships
walkthrough.text: Ships are constructed at the Wharf or Shipping Line buildings by paying the required resources. Wooden ships cost wood, while iron ships cost iron and steel. Ships are critical because they serve two purposes: they are worth victory points at game end, and they provide food during feeding rounds. A wooden ship typically provides two food per harvest.

walkthrough.title: Selling Goods
walkthrough.text: You can sell goods for Francs at the Marketplace or similar buildings. Each good has a fixed sale price. Francs count directly toward your final wealth score, so selling goods converts resources into guaranteed points. However, you might get more value by using those goods to construct buildings or ships instead.

walkthrough.title: Feeding Rounds
walkthrough.text: At specific points during the game marked on the round overview cards, a feeding phase occurs. You must pay a food cost to feed your workers. The food requirement starts at two and increases as the game progresses. Food can come from Francs, fish, smoked fish, bread, meat, or other food-valued goods.

walkthrough.title: Ships Reduce Feeding Pressure
walkthrough.text: This is why ships are so important. Each ship you own provides a permanent food supply during every feeding round. A wooden ship gives two food per harvest, and iron ships give even more. Once you have enough ships, feeding becomes trivial and you can focus entirely on building wealth.

walkthrough.title: Loans
walkthrough.text: If you cannot pay your full food cost during a feeding round, you must take loan cards to cover the shortfall. Each loan card is worth negative seven Francs at game end. That is a brutal penalty. You can repay a loan at any time during your turn by paying five Francs, which removes the card. Sometimes a well-timed loan is worth it to grab a key building, but avoid them if you can.

walkthrough.title: Special Buildings
walkthrough.text: The special buildings dealt during setup create a unique landscape every game. Some offer powerful conversion chains not available through standard buildings. Others provide unique ways to score victory points. Always check what special buildings are available at the start of the game, as they can heavily influence your strategy.

walkthrough.title: Late-Game Buildings
walkthrough.text: Some buildings only enter play in later rounds, as indicated by the round overview cards. These tend to be expensive but very powerful. The Church and Town Hall, for example, are high-value buildings that can swing final scores significantly. Plan ahead for these if you see them coming.

walkthrough.title: Game End
walkthrough.text: The game ends after the final round, which is determined by player count. There is one last feeding phase at the end. After that, everyone tallies up their total wealth.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: Your final score is calculated by adding three things and subtracting one. Add all the Francs you have in hand. Add the printed value of every building you own. Add the printed value of every ship you own. Then subtract seven Francs for each outstanding loan card. Leftover goods in your supply are worth nothing. Only Francs, buildings, ships, and loans matter for scoring.

walkthrough.title: Determining the Winner
walkthrough.text: The player with the highest total wealth wins. If there is a tie, the player with the greater number of goods in their supply wins. Le Havre rewards the player who most efficiently converted raw materials into permanent wealth over the course of the game.

Goal: most total wealth (Francs + buildings + ships - loans)

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Le Havre
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through the opening turns of a three-player game with Player 1, Player 2, and Player 3. Each player starts with five Francs and one charcoal. Follow along with the moves to learn how the game flows.

walkthrough.title: The Opening Supply
walkthrough.text: Before the first turn, goods have been placed on the supply spaces according to the round one card. You can see small piles of fish, wood, clay, and iron on the offer spaces. These will grow each turn as more goods arrive at the harbor. Let's see how the first few turns play out.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Taking Goods
walkthrough.text: Player 1 goes first. They advance the supply arrow and add the indicated goods to the board. Then for their main action, they take all the goods from the wood offer space, collecting three wood. A nice haul of resources to start the game. Wood is versatile because it can be used for building, processed into charcoal, or even used as energy.

walkthrough.title: Player 2: Also Taking Goods
walkthrough.text: Player 2 advances the supply arrow and adds more goods. Then they take all the fish from the fish offer space. Fish are useful because they can be eaten as food during feeding rounds or processed into smoked fish at the Smokehouse for more value.

walkthrough.title: Player 3: Using a Building
walkthrough.text: They place their worker on the Construction Firm and pay wood and clay to construct an early building. This building goes into their personal area. Player 3 now owns something worth victory points at game end.

walkthrough.title: Why Building Early Can Pay Off
walkthrough.text: Constructing a building on your first turn is aggressive but can be rewarding. You immediately gain a building worth victory points. If opponents want to use your building, they pay you an entry fee. The risk is that you spent resources early and might struggle with feeding later.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Processing Wood
walkthrough.text: Charcoal is incredibly versatile. It counts as energy for processing buildings, it can be used as food during feeding rounds, and it is needed for various construction costs. This was an efficient move.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Ignoring Feeding
walkthrough.text: Player 2 has been collecting goods and processing them, but has completely forgotten about the upcoming feeding round. They have lots of iron and clay but almost nothing they can eat. The feeding round is only two turns away, and Player 2 needs to scramble to find food or face taking a loan.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Track Feeding
walkthrough.text: This is the most common beginner mistake in Le Havre. Always check the round overview cards to see when the next feeding round is coming. Count backward from that point. If you do not have enough food or food-producing resources, you need to start planning two or three turns in advance. Getting caught with no food means taking a loan worth negative seven points.

walkthrough.title: Player 2: Emergency Food Run
walkthrough.text: Player 2 panics and uses the Marketplace to sell some iron for Francs. Francs can be spent as food during feeding rounds at a one-to-one rate. This works, but selling raw iron for Francs is inefficient. Player 2 could have gotten much more value by processing that iron into steel first. Desperation makes you inefficient.

walkthrough.title: Player 3: Building a Ship
walkthrough.text: This ship is worth victory points and provides two food during every future feeding round. Player 3 has just solved their food problem for a significant portion of the game.

walkthrough.title: Why Ships Are So Valuable
walkthrough.text: Let's do the math on ships. A wooden ship provides two food every feeding round. Over the course of the game, that could be eight to twelve food you do not have to worry about. Without a ship, you would spend multiple turns collecting and preparing food. Ships free up your turns to focus on building wealth.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Hoarding Raw Goods
walkthrough.text: Player 1 has been collecting wood, clay, and iron every chance they get. Their supply is overflowing with raw materials. But here is the problem: raw goods are worth absolutely zero at game end. Only Francs, building values, and ship values count. Player 1 is sitting on a mountain of potential that is not yet converted to actual wealth.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Process and Build, Do Not Hoard
walkthrough.text: Every raw good in your supply at game end is a wasted opportunity. Wood should become charcoal or buildings. Clay should become bricks. Iron should become steel or ships. Always be thinking about your conversion pipeline. Taking goods is only valuable if you have a plan to turn them into points.

walkthrough.title: The Feeding Round Arrives
walkthrough.text: Each player must pay two food. Player 3 has their ship providing two food, so they are completely covered with no cost. Player 1 spends their charcoal as food. Player 2 spends two of their Francs. Everyone survived, but Player 2 and Player 1 spent actual resources while Player 3 paid nothing from their reserves.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Taking Small Piles
walkthrough.text: Player 2 sees two fish sitting on an offer space and grabs them. Meanwhile, the clay offer space has been accumulating for five turns and now has six clay on it. Player 3 swoops in next turn and takes that massive pile. Taking small piles when large ones are building up is a tempo mistake that costs you dearly.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Watch the Offer Spaces
walkthrough.text: Keep your eye on which offer spaces are growing. A pile of six or seven goods is an incredible windfall for one turn's action. If you see a big pile building, consider whether you should grab it before an opponent does. Sometimes taking goods you do not urgently need is better than letting someone else get a massive haul.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Forgetting Entry Fees
walkthrough.text: Player 1 excitedly uses Player 3's building to process some goods. But they forgot to pay the entry fee! In Le Havre, you must pay the building owner one food or one Franc whenever you use their building. This is not optional. Make sure to check building ownership before committing to an action. That one Franc entry fee might make another building a better choice.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Factor In Entry Fees
walkthrough.text: Before using any building, check who owns it. If it is your own or a neutral town building, it is free. If another player owns it, you will pay an entry fee. Over the course of the game, these fees add up. This is why owning key buildings yourself is so valuable. You save on fees and earn income when others use your buildings.

walkthrough.title: Mid-Game Strategy Check
walkthrough.text: Let's check where our players stand. Player 3 is in the strongest position. They have a ship for food, they own a building for points and income, and they have been processing goods efficiently. Player 1 has raw materials but needs to convert them. Player 2 is scrambling and has already spent Francs on food that could have gone toward buildings.

walkthrough.title: The Comeback Path
walkthrough.text: Even if you fall behind early, Le Havre offers comeback opportunities. Late-game buildings like the Church and Town Hall are extremely valuable. Special buildings can provide unique scoring paths. And a well-timed loan to grab a critical building can pay for itself many times over. The game rewards long-term planning over short-term gains.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have practiced the core flow of Le Havre. You have seen how to take goods, use buildings, process resources, build ships, and navigate feeding rounds. You have also seen four common mistakes: ignoring feeding schedules, hoarding raw goods, taking small piles when big ones are available, and forgetting entry fees. You are ready to play a full game.

walkthrough.title: Before You Set Sail
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics and common pitfalls to avoid.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to buildings, goods, and game terms.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Before You Set Sail

Reference the Appendix → quick-reference for buildings and terms
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we talk strategy, here are the most common mistakes new Le Havre players make. First, ignoring the feeding schedule and getting caught without food, forcing you to take devastating loans. Second, hoarding raw goods that are worth zero points at game end instead of processing or building with them. Third, taking small piles of goods when larger piles are accumulating on other offer spaces. Fourth, spending too many Francs on entry fees when you could build the key buildings yourself. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the biggest beginner traps.

walkthrough.title: Build a Ship Early
walkthrough.text: The single best thing you can do in your first game is build a ship as early as possible. A wooden ship provides two food every feeding round for the rest of the game. That is eight to twelve food you do not have to spend turns collecting. Without a ship, you will burn multiple turns gathering fish, grain, or Francs just to survive each feeding round. With a ship, those turns go toward building wealth instead. Make getting to the Wharf a top priority.

walkthrough.title: Plan Your Processing Chains
walkthrough.text: Raw goods are the currency of the early game, but processed goods are what build your engine. Learn the key chains: wood becomes charcoal at the Charburner, clay becomes bricks at the Brickworks, iron becomes steel at the Steel Mill. Each step up the chain unlocks better buildings and more valuable construction. The most successful players have a clear processing pipeline. They know which raw goods they need, which buildings they will use to process them, and what they will build with the results.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Supply Cycle
walkthrough.text: Goods arrive at the harbor in a predictable rotation. The supply arrow cycles through the seven spaces, and you can see exactly what will appear next. Use this knowledge to your advantage. If you know iron is coming on the next supply step, and the iron space already has some, wait one more turn and grab the bigger pile. Timing your takes for maximum haul is one of the most important skills in Le Havre.

walkthrough.title: Do Not Fear Loans
walkthrough.text: Loans cost negative seven Francs at game end, which sounds terrible. But sometimes taking a loan is the right play. If a loan lets you build a building worth twenty Francs that you would otherwise miss, you net thirteen points. The key is to take loans deliberately for investment, not accidentally because you forgot about feeding. A well-timed loan to grab the Steel Mill or Church can win you the game.

walkthrough.title: Own Key Buildings
walkthrough.text: Building ownership is a two-way advantage. You use your buildings for free, saving entry fees every time. And opponents pay you whenever they need to use them. The most contested buildings are processing buildings everyone needs, like the Brickworks, Steel Mill, and Smokehouse. If you own the Brickworks and everyone needs bricks for their buildings, you collect a Franc every time they visit. Over the course of a game, that adds up to substantial income.

walkthrough.title: Count the Food Before Feeding
walkthrough.text: Every time a feeding round approaches, count exactly what food you have available. Remember that Francs can be spent as food at a one-to-one ratio. Charcoal counts as food. Ships provide automatic food. If you are short, you have a few turns to fix it. If you are exactly right, you might still want a buffer because an opponent could take the goods you were counting on. Always know your food situation two turns before a feeding round.

walkthrough.title: Value Your Turns
walkthrough.text: You have roughly twenty turns in the entire game. Every single turn matters. Taking two fish from an offer space when there is a pile of five clay on another space is not just a small mistake. It is wasting five percent of your total actions. Think of each turn as a limited resource. Would you rather grab a small pile of goods, or use a building to make a key conversion? The most efficient players squeeze maximum value from every single action.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't ignore feeding schedule — loans cost -7 Francs each
Don't hoard raw goods — they score 0 at game end
Don't take small piles when big ones are available
Don't pay entry fees when you could own key buildings

summary.title: Build a Ship Early
bullets: Wooden ship = 2 food/feeding for the rest of the game
Saves 8-12 food over the game (multiple turns freed up)
Without a ship, turns are wasted collecting food
Make reaching the Wharf a top early priority

summary.title: Plan Your Processing Chains
bullets: Wood → Charcoal, Clay → Bricks, Iron → Steel
Processed goods unlock better buildings and higher value
Know your pipeline: what raw goods → which buildings → what to build
Processing = the engine that drives your entire strategy

summary.title: Watch the Supply Cycle
bullets: Supply arrow rotates predictably through 7 spaces
You can see what goods are coming next
Wait for bigger piles before taking — timing is key
A pile of 6-7 goods in one action = massive efficiency

summary.title: Do Not Fear Loans
bullets: Loans = -7 Francs, but a 20-Franc building = +13 net
Take loans deliberately for investment, not by accident
Well-timed loan for Steel Mill or Church can win the game
Repay for 5 Francs when you can afford it

summary.title: Own Key Buildings
bullets: Own buildings = free to use + earn entry fees from opponents
Priority targets: Brickworks, Steel Mill, Smokehouse
Entry fee income accumulates significantly over the game
Two-way advantage: save money AND earn money

summary.title: Count the Food Before Feeding
bullets: Count exact food available 2 turns before feeding
Francs = food at 1:1, charcoal counts as food, ships auto-feed
Keep a buffer — opponents can take goods you were counting on
Exact planning prevents panic and loans

summary.title: Value Your Turns
bullets: ~20 total turns in the game — each one is 5% of your actions
Small piles when big piles are available = wasted turns
Maximize value per action: process, build, or take big piles
Most efficient players squeeze every turn for maximum value

walkthrough.title: Ship Rush Strategy
walkthrough.text: The ship rush is one of the most powerful openings in Le Havre. The goal is to build wooden ships as fast as possible, ideally getting your first ship before the first feeding round. Ships solve your food problem permanently and generate victory points. Rush to the Wharf with enough wood, build a ship, and suddenly you have zero feeding pressure. With food solved, every remaining turn can go toward pure wealth generation. The risk is a slow start on building ownership, but the tempo gain from never worrying about food is enormous.

walkthrough.title: Building Empire Strategy
walkthrough.text: The building empire strategy focuses on buying as many buildings as possible for their printed values and entry fee income. The key is to target buildings that opponents will need to use frequently. If you own the Brickworks and the Steel Mill, you earn a Franc every time someone processes clay or iron. Over twenty turns, that passive income adds up significantly. The building empire also scores well because building values are typically higher than ship values for the same resource investment.

walkthrough.title: Processing Chain Mastery
walkthrough.text: This strategy maximizes the value of every raw good you collect by building efficient conversion chains. The core idea is to never sell raw goods or use them directly when you could process them first. Wood becomes charcoal, which becomes energy for turning iron into steel, which goes into iron ships worth thirty or more points. Clay becomes bricks, which fund expensive buildings. Every step up the chain multiplies your value. The master processor turns three wood and two iron into one iron ship worth more than the sum of its parts.

walkthrough.title: Timing the Offer Spaces
walkthrough.text: Advanced players track the supply cycle religiously. Because the arrow rotates predictably through seven spaces, you can calculate exactly when each good type will be added next. If iron was just added and the iron space has four tokens, you know it will be several turns before more iron arrives. Grabbing it now versus waiting depends on whether opponents also need iron. The advanced move is to take goods that are about to stop accumulating, and let goods you need keep growing until the last possible moment.

walkthrough.title: Strategic Loan Management
walkthrough.text: Expert Le Havre players treat loans as a financial tool, not a failure. Taking a loan costs negative seven at game end, but repaying costs only five Francs during the game. If you take a loan on turn three to build a building worth twenty-four Francs, and you repay the loan by turn ten, you spent five Francs to gain twenty-four Francs of building value. That is a nineteen-point swing. The key rules: never take accidental loans from poor feeding planning, only take deliberate loans for high-value investments, and always repay before game end if possible.

walkthrough.title: Denying Opponents Resources
walkthrough.text: Le Havre has a strong interactive element through resource denial. If you see an opponent building toward iron ships, taking the iron pile before they can denies them a critical turn of progress. If someone needs the Brickworks and you can occupy it first, you force them to wait or use a less efficient building. Watch what your opponents are collecting and what buildings they own. Taking a large pile of goods you do not urgently need can be correct if it prevents an opponent from getting a massive haul.

walkthrough.title: Late-Game Power Buildings
walkthrough.text: The most valuable buildings in Le Havre appear in the later rounds. The Church, Town Hall, and various special buildings can be worth twenty to thirty Francs or more. Planning your resource pipeline to afford these buildings when they appear is what separates good players from great ones. Start collecting bricks and steel in the mid-game even if you do not have an immediate use for them. When that thirty-Franc building appears in round six, you want to be ready to build it before anyone else.

walkthrough.title: Reading Special Buildings
walkthrough.text: At the start of every game, study the special buildings that were dealt. These create a unique strategic landscape. Some games have powerful food-generation specials that make ship rushing less necessary. Other games have incredible processing buildings that reward a chain strategy. Still others have high-value specials that reward a building empire approach. The best players adapt their strategy to the specific special buildings available rather than following the same plan every game.

summary.title: Ship Rush Strategy
bullets: Build wooden ships ASAP — ideally before first feeding round
Ships solve food permanently + generate VP
Zero feeding pressure → all remaining turns = pure wealth
Risk: slow start on building ownership

summary.title: Building Empire Strategy
bullets: Buy many buildings for printed values + entry fee income
Target buildings opponents need: Brickworks, Steel Mill, Smokehouse
Passive income from entry fees adds up over 20 turns
Building values typically higher than ship values per resource

summary.title: Processing Chain Mastery
bullets: Never sell/use raw goods when processing is available
Wood → Charcoal → energy → Steel → Iron Ships
Clay → Bricks → expensive buildings
Each processing step multiplies value

summary.title: Timing the Offer Spaces
bullets: Track supply cycle — arrow rotates predictably through 7 spaces
Calculate when each good type will be added next
Take goods about to stop accumulating
Let needed goods keep growing until last possible moment

summary.title: Strategic Loan Management
bullets: Loans as financial tool: -7 end-game, but repay for 5 during game
Example: loan on turn 3 → 24-Franc building = +19 swing
Never accidental loans — only deliberate high-value investments
Always repay before game end if possible

summary.title: Denying Opponents Resources
bullets: Take iron pile to block opponent's ship plans
Occupy contested buildings to force opponents to wait
Watch what opponents collect and what they're building toward
Taking unneeded goods can be correct to deny a massive haul

summary.title: Late-Game Power Buildings
bullets: Church, Town Hall, specials can be worth 20-30+ Francs
Stockpile bricks and steel in mid-game for late-game buildings
First player to build the power building gets a huge score swing
Plan resource pipeline 2-3 rounds ahead

summary.title: Reading Special Buildings
bullets: Study special buildings at game start — they shape strategy
Food-generation specials → less need for ship rush
Powerful processing specials → reward chain strategy
Adapt your plan to available specials each game

entries.definition: The currency of Le Havre. Francs count directly as victory points at game end. Also usable as food during feeding rounds at a 1:1 ratio. Earned by selling goods at the Marketplace or other buildings.
A basic good that arrives at the harbor. Fish can be eaten as food (1 food value) or processed into Smoked Fish at the Smokehouse for greater value.
A basic good used for building construction, ship building, and as energy. Can be processed into Charcoal at the Charburner. One of the most versatile resources in the game.
A basic good used in some building construction. Becomes far more valuable when processed into Bricks at the Brickworks. Required for many mid-game buildings.
A basic good that can be processed into Steel at the Steel Mill (requires energy). Iron and Steel are essential for building iron ships, which are the most valuable ships in the game.
A basic good that can be processed into Bread at the Bakehouse (requires energy). Bread is an efficient food source worth 2 food. Grain can also be used to feed cattle.
A basic good that can be processed into Meat and Hides at the Abattoir. Cattle can also be used directly as 3 food during feeding. Hides can be further processed into Leather at the Tannery.
A processed good made from Fish at the Smokehouse (requires energy). Worth more as food than raw fish and can be sold for a better price at the Marketplace.

A processed good made from Wood at the Charburner. Serves triple duty: counts as energy for processing buildings, counts as food (3 food value), and is used in some building costs. Extremely versatile.
A processed good made from Clay at the Brickworks. Essential for constructing many mid-game and late-game buildings. One of the most important processed goods to have access to.
A processed good made from Iron at the Steel Mill (requires energy). Required for building iron ships, which are the most valuable ships in the game. Also used in some expensive building costs.
A processed good made from Grain at the Bakehouse (requires energy). Worth 2 food, making it one of the most efficient food sources in the game.
A processed good obtained from Cattle at the Abattoir. Worth food and can be sold at the Marketplace. Processing cattle also yields Hides.
A byproduct of processing Cattle at the Abattoir. Can be further processed into Leather at the Tannery for additional value.
A processed good made from Hides at the Tannery. A high-value good that can be sold for a good price at the Marketplace.
An advanced processed good that serves as highly efficient energy. Can be used in place of charcoal or wood as energy for processing buildings. Produced at specialized buildings.
A resource type required by several processing buildings (Steel Mill, Smokehouse, Bakehouse). Energy can be provided by Wood, Charcoal, or Coke tokens. Each has different efficiency levels.
The marker on the game board that tracks which supply space is filled next. Advanced one space at the start of every turn. Cycles through seven positions, making the supply cycle predictable.

The spaces on the board where goods accumulate. Each corresponds to a good type. Goods pile up if not taken, allowing players to grab large hauls in a single action. Taking goods clears the entire space.

The predictable rotation of goods arriving at the harbor. The supply arrow moves through seven positions, and each position adds specific goods. Advanced players track this cycle to time their takes optimally.
One of seven cards showing what happens each round: which goods appear in the supply, when feeding rounds occur, and when new ships become available. Essential for forward planning.
A mandatory phase at specific intervals where each player must pay a food cost. The cost starts at 2 and increases later. Ships provide automatic food. Failure to pay means taking Loan cards.

The cost to use a building owned by another player, typically 1 food or 1 Franc (printed on the building card). Your own buildings and neutral town buildings are free. Entry fees are paid directly to the building owner.
Each player's single colored marker placed on buildings when using them. Only one worker can occupy a building at a time. When displaced by another player, the marker returns to its owner.

Taken when a player cannot pay their full food cost during a feeding round. Each Loan is worth -7 Francs at game end. Can be repaid at any time during your turn for 5 Francs. Strategic loans for key investments can be worthwhile.
One of the two starting town buildings. Allows you to construct a building from the available supply by paying its resource cost. The built building goes to your personal area.
One of the two starting town buildings. An alternative way to construct buildings, often with slightly different costs or capabilities than the Construction Firm.
A standard building that converts Wood into Charcoal. One of the most important early-game buildings because Charcoal is versatile (energy, food, construction).
A standard building that converts Clay into Bricks. Bricks are needed for many mid-game and late-game buildings. Owning the Brickworks gives a significant strategic advantage.
A standard building that converts Iron into Steel (requires energy). Steel is essential for iron ships and some expensive buildings. One of the most contested buildings to own.
A standard building that converts Fish into Smoked Fish (requires energy). Smoked Fish is worth more as food and sells for a better price than raw Fish.
A standard building that converts Grain into Bread (requires energy). Bread is worth 2 food, making it one of the most efficient food sources available.
A standard building that converts Cattle into Meat and Hides. Meat provides food value, and Hides can be processed further into Leather at the Tannery.
A standard building that converts Hides into Leather. Part of the Cattle processing chain: Cattle → Meat + Hides → Leather.
A building where you can sell goods for Francs at fixed rates. Useful for converting surplus goods into points, but processing goods first usually yields better prices.
A building used to construct ships by paying the required resources (wood for wooden ships, iron/steel for iron ships). Ships provide VP and automatic food during feeding rounds.
An alternative building for constructing ships, sometimes with different costs or options than the Wharf. May become available in later rounds.
One of the 33 buildings that appear in every game of Le Havre. These include all the basic processing buildings, the Marketplace, Wharf, and more. Sorted by number in the display.

One of 36 unique buildings shuffled and partially dealt each game. These vary from session to session, creating different strategic landscapes. Some offer powerful unique abilities not found in standard buildings.
A neutral building not owned by any player. The Construction Firm and Building Firm start as town buildings. Town buildings are free to use — no entry fee required.
The basic ship type, constructed at the Wharf using wood. Provides moderate VP and 2 food per feeding round. Essential for managing feeding pressure throughout the game.
An advanced ship type requiring iron and/or steel to construct. Worth more VP than wooden ships and provides more food per feeding round. The premium option for ship builders.
The most expensive and valuable ship in Le Havre. Requires significant steel investment to build. Worth the highest VP of any ship and provides substantial food per feeding round.

The Franc value printed on each building card. This counts directly toward your final score for buildings you own. Building values range from a few Francs for basic buildings to 20-30+ for late-game power buildings.
The Franc value printed on each ship. Counts directly toward your final score. Combined with the food benefit, ships are among the most efficient scoring investments in the game.
Your final score in Le Havre. Calculated as: Francs in hand + total building values + total ship values - (7 per outstanding Loan). Leftover raw and processed goods are worth zero.
A high-value late-game building worth significant Francs. Typically one of the most valuable buildings in the game. Requires substantial resources to construct but can swing final scores.
Another high-value late-game building. Like the Church, it appears in later rounds and requires expensive resources. Building it before opponents can provide a decisive scoring advantage.
